attributes.stem:
A 56-year-old man presents with a rapidly worsening itchy rash on his chest and arms. This started about two months ago, but over the last week blisters have started to appear. Ruptured bullae leave behind erosions and crusts. He does not have any significant medical history and does not take any medications.

On examination multiple tense bullae are seen on an erythematous and oedematous skin (Image). Nikolsky's sign is negative. A skin biopsy is performed, which shows subepidermal blistering with eosinophil-rich infiltrate and linear deposition of IgG and C3 along the basement membrane on direct immunofluorescence (DIF) testing.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This clinical picture is consistent with a diagnosis of bullous pemphigoid. Bullous pemphigoid is typically characterised by pruritus and tense bullae usually arising on erythematous inflamed skin in a symmetric distribution (flexural surfaces of the limbs, inner thighs, abdomen) and rarely with mucosal involvement and atrophic scarring. Prior to blisters developing, pre-bullous pemphigoid often consists of an erythematous eczematous or urticated eruption that is very pruritic. The biopsy findings in this patient are also consistent with bullous pemphigoid. Oral prednisolone is often first-line management for moderate to severe disease. Dosing could start at 0.5mg/kg daily and the dose would be weaned depending on disease control. If oral steroids are unable to be weaned off, steroid-sparing agents (e.g. methotrexate, dapsone, mycophenolate) could be considered. It is important to consider the patient's comorbidities when prescribing prednisolone considering dose adjustments and contraindications. Topical steroids are a useful adjunct and can be used without oral steroids in mild diseases.

Cephalexin is not an appropriate choice for the treatment of this patient's condition because the clinical presentation and diagnostic findings are indicative of bullous pemphigoid, which is an autoimmune blistering skin disorder. Cephalexin is an antibiotic and is not usually effective in the treatment of autoimmune blistering disorders like bullous pemphigoid.

Dapsone is used in some countries most often in combination with topical corticosteroids, but its efficacy is controversial. Dapsone may be considered, in particular, in patients with contraindications to oral clobetasol propionate or immunosuppressive treatments, with mild and moderate.

Methotrexate, an immunosuppressant, has been used in the treatment of bullous pemphigoid, particularly in patients who are unable to tolerate systemic corticosteroids or require steroid-sparing agents. It is often used in combination with systemic corticosteroids. However, it is not used at this stage of the disease.

Topical mupirocin can be used for treating dermatological bacterial infections such as impetigo, but it has no use in the treatment of bullous pemphigoid because the underlying etiology is not a bacterial infection.
learningPoints: Oral prednisolone is the treatment of choice in moderate-severe forms of bullous pemphigoid.
references: Borradori L, Van Beek N, Feliciani C, et al. Updated S2 K guidelines for the management of bullous pemphigoid initiated by the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology (EADV). J Eur Acad Dermatol Venereol. 2022;36(10):1689-1704. doi:10.1111/jdv.18220
